Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep → Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling → Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery

Lesson 1 of 3

Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery

Start with the thin vertical slice that proves the model, then add interfaces, policies, and edge cases in an order the interviewer can follow.

20-30 min LLD flow Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling 4 guided stops

Study map

What this lesson helps you explain

A clean LLD interview is not only about the final class diagram. It is also about sequencing implementation choices so the interviewer sees judgment rather than rushed coding.

Start here Frame the trade-off before diving into architecture. 1. Open with the core slice Pick one representative use case to implement first2. Narrate what comes next Call out planned extension points while the code is still small3. Leave the design reviewable Prefer clarity over cleverness

Guided walkthrough

Move through the lesson in a clear interview order

4 steps

Start with the framing, walk the concept in order, then use the right rail for prompts, pitfalls, and visual anchors.

1. Interview framing A clean LLD interview is not only about the final class diagram. It is also about sequencing implementation choices so the interviewer sees judgment rather than rushed coding. 2. Open with the core slice Pick one representative use case to implement first3. Narrate what comes next Call out planned extension points while the code is still small4. Leave the design reviewable Prefer clarity over cleverness
1

Start with this framing

Interview framing

A clean LLD interview is not only about the final class diagram. It is also about sequencing implementation choices so the interviewer sees judgment rather than rushed coding.

What to say first

  • Implement one end-to-end happy path first.
  • State where extension points will land before over-abstracting.
  • Keep class and method names tied to domain behavior.
2

Study section

Open with the core slice

Write the smallest set of types that supports one main scenario end to end before filling in optional abstractions or storage adapters.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Pick one representative use case to implement first
  • Introduce classes in dependency order so the story stays readable
  • Defer non-critical features until the main flow works
3

Study section

Narrate what comes next

As you code, state where validation, interfaces, tests, or follow-up requirements would attach so the interviewer sees a roadmap beyond the first draft.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Call out planned extension points while the code is still small
  • Mention what you would stub versus fully implement under time pressure
  • Keep the public API stable while internals evolve
4

Study section

Leave the design reviewable

Readable names, small methods, and explicit test seams matter more in the interview than squeezing every concern into one pass.

Call these points out explicitly

  • Prefer clarity over cleverness
  • Keep construction and business logic separate
  • Stop to summarize trade-offs after each meaningful milestone

Checklist

Things to say clearly

  • Implement one end-to-end happy path first.
  • State where extension points will land before over-abstracting.
  • Keep class and method names tied to domain behavior.
  • Summarize the design after each incremental milestone.

Common pitfalls

What to avoid

  • Trying to code every anticipated requirement before the first scenario works.
  • Dumping all implementation into a single service class for speed.
  • Using vague placeholders without explaining how the design will evolve.

Interview prompts

Practice aloud

  • What do you code first in a 45-minute machine-coding round?
  • How do you show extensibility without burning time on abstractions?
  • When should you pause and explain rather than continue typing?

Related topics

Keep the thread going

Problem framing and requirements Foundations and estimation

Interactive practice lab

Write, save, and move through the mock interview

Answers stay in your browser so you can come back later.

PRACTICE GUIDE

Explain the concept Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Explain the concept

Teach "Machine-coding skeletons and incremental delivery" back to an interviewer. Summarize what it is, when you would use it, and why it matters in a real system.

Goal

Explain the topic in interviewer-friendly language before diving into implementation details.

Guardrails

  • What do you code first in a 45-minute machine-coding round?
  • Open with the core slice
  • Narrate what comes next
  • Implement one end-to-end happy path first.

Suggested Structure

  • Definition
  • When to use it
  • Benefits
  • Trade-offs
